Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ya Sin (1)

This is from among the abbreviated letters (Hurufe Muqatta'at), upon which a comprehensive discussion has passed at the beginning of the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. This is the Qur'anic name of this Surah. Some people have interpreted its meaning as 'O human!' (Ya Ayyuha al-Insan), but this statement is entirely without proof.

"By the wise Qur'an, Indeed you are of the messengers." (2-3)

'Wa' (By) is in the sense of an oath, and an oath in Arabic, as our teacher Maulana Farahi (ra) clarified in his book 'Al-Im'an fi Aqsam al-Qur'an', is used for testimony. The meaning is: this wise Qur'an which you are reciting to the people is in itself sufficient testimony to the fact that you belong to the ranks of the messengers. No one other than a messenger is capable of presenting such a wise and miraculous discourse. From this, it is learned that in the miraculous nature (i'jaz) of the Qur'an, the primary factor is its wisdom and its philosophy. The eloquence and grandeur of its language are supplementary to it.

"On a straight path." (4)

This is a subsequent predicate (khabar) after the first, and its occurrence without a coordinating conjunction (harf-e-atf) is proof that this wise Qur'an testifies to both matters simultaneously: to the fact that you are among the messengers of Allah, and also to the fact that you are on an entirely straight path and are inviting people to walk upon this straight path. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Sirat' (path) is to magnify its status, implying that this is the path of reason and human nature, and a highly straight path shown by God. Those who are avoiding this straight path have distorted their own nature and abandoned their intellect, and for this reason, a straight thing appears crooked to them.

"A revelation of the Exalted in Might, the Merciful." (5)

'Tanzeel' (Revelation) is in the accusative case (mansoob) due to an omitted verb. We have explained its meaning in another place—that it is used for sending down something gradually and with highly elaborate care. This draws attention to another aspect of the Qur'an—that the Exalted in Might and Merciful God has sent it down with great care and graduation so that people may reflect upon it, understand it, and receive guidance to the straight path from it. Here, two attributes of Allah Almighty are referred to: first, 'the Exalted in Might' (Al-Aziz), and second, 'the Merciful' (Al-Raheem). One of these attributes is for warning and the other for glad tidings. The meaning is: those who deny it should remember that this is not the request of a petitioner, but rather the command of an Exalted and Powerful Master which must be obeyed, and Who will necessarily punish those who rebel. At the same time, He is Merciful, and it is for this mercy of His that He has sent down this Book; so those servants of Allah who value this Qur'an will be blessed by Him with His endless mercies.

"That you may warn a people whose forefathers were not warned, so they are heedless." (6)

This describes the objective of sending down the Qur'an—stating that Allah has sent it down with this arrangement so that you may warn those among whom no warner was sent before you and who were lying in heedlessness, fully informing them of the end of life. This points to the Children of Ismail and describes the great favor which Allah Almighty bestowed upon the unlettered Arabs in accordance with the supplication of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and the prophecies of the Prophets. In this lies encouragement for them, along with the warning that if they do not value this blessing, they will prepare for themselves the greatest misery instead of the greatest success. This same theme is expressed in other places as follows: 'That you may warn a people to whom no warner has come before you...' (Al-Qasas: 46).

"The word has already come into effect upon most of them, so they do not believe." (7)

'The word' points to that statement of Allah Almighty which He uttered in reply to Iblis when he threatened: 'I will surely lead the majority of the progeny of Adam astray.' At that time, Allah Almighty said: 'I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together.' Referring to this word, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled—stating that your duty is to warn, so continue doing that, but do not expect that every single person among them will accept your invitation; rather, there are many among them upon whom Our word has come into effect, who will become the fuel of Hell for their crime of following Iblis. Such people will not believe. There is no need to pursue them or worry over them.

"Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks, and they are to their chins, so they are with heads kept aloft." (8)

This is a depiction of the arrogance of those who do not believe—showing that it is as if there are shackles on their necks that reach up to their chins, because of which their heads remain held aloft in such a way that they can neither bend downwards nor look upwards. The result is that they can neither observe the signs of the earth nor look at the wonders of the sky. Allah Almighty has attributed this condition of theirs to Himself, stating: 'We have made them so.' The reason, as we have been pointing out in various places, is that this state of any individual or group occurs in accordance with the law of Allah. Those who deliberately adopt the path of deviation and aversion from the truth, and spend their lives closing their eyes to the signs of Allah, Allah subjects them to their own desires and deeds, the result of which is that no teaching or warning can affect them. This reality is pointed out in: 'No! Rather, the stain has covered their hearts of that which they were earning' (Al-Mutaffifin: 14).

'Muqmahoon' (with heads kept aloft) is originally used for a camel whose head has been tied backward in such a way that its neck cannot move below or above a certain limit. Exactly the same is the case with a person on whose neck an iron shackle is put. He too can neither lower his head nor raise it, but his neck remains tense at a specific angle. As we have pointed out, this is a depiction of the arrogant. Such people remain unaware of all the signs before them, behind them, and to their right and left. They never pay attention to anything except their own 'ego'.

"And We have put before them a barrier and behind them a barrier and covered them, so they do not see." (9)

This explains the preceding theme—stating that in this way, We have set up a barrier before them and behind them, and placed a veil over their eyes, because of which they cannot see anything. Wonder over this condition of theirs is expressed in Surah Saba: 'Have they not looked at what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth?' (9). Regarding such arrogant people, it should also be remembered that they are slaves of their past traditions and their future interests. Both of these things become a barrier in their path, to the extent that they do not remain capable of seeing anything beyond them.

"And it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them - they will not believe." (10)

Obviously, such people are afflicted with a severe intellectual and moral disease; therefore, warning them or not warning them is entirely useless in terms of the primary objective. These people are not going to believe. If they are warned, it is merely to establish final proof (itmam al-hujjah) so that they cannot present any excuse on the Day of Judgment. Have a look at the discussion on the sealing of hearts that has passed at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah.

"You can only warn one who follows the message and fears the Most Merciful unseen. So give him glad tidings of forgiveness and a noble reward." (11)

That is, your teaching and warning can be effective only upon those who listen to your advice, reflect upon it, and open the doors of their hearts to it, and also utilize their intellect instead of living as slaves to physical sensations—who are otherwise not ready to accept anything until they are shown the punishment. Credible faith is that which is brought by utilizing the capabilities of hearing, sight, intellect, and heart, not after seeing the realities with one's eyes. It states: leave the matter of those who want to believe only after seeing everything with their eyes to Allah. However, give glad tidings of forgiveness and a noble reward to those who fear the Most Merciful while He is unseen.

We have clarified in various places that the fear of God is, in reality, a requirement of His being the Most Merciful (Al-Rahman). He is the Most Merciful; therefore, it is necessary that He rewards the righteous for their righteousness and punishes the wicked for their wickedness. It is for the manifestation of this mercy of His that He has appointed the Day of Judgment, in which His perfect mercy and perfect justice will stand manifest.

Ayah 12

This is a reminder of that Day of Judgment which is the logical requirement of His mercy. It states that a Day is about to come when We will raise all the dead, and We are recording whatever they have sent ahead and whatever traces they have left behind. The objective is to highlight its necessary consequence—that when We are going to raise everyone and are also recording everyone's deeds, We will necessarily deal with everyone according to their deeds. This logical consequence is not explicitly mentioned here. One reason is that it is obvious even without being mentioned, and the second is that regarding the Resurrection, the primary doubt of the deniers was from two aspects only. First, they considered it improbable to be raised alive again after death. Second, they wondered who could possibly preserve all the words and deeds of such a vast world to sit down and reckon them one day. Both of these doubts are cleared here, after which the logical consequence automatically emerges.

'and what they have sent ahead and their traces' expresses the same theme that is stated in another place with the words: 'Man will be informed that Day of what he sent ahead and kept back'. In this world, a person does many deeds, according to their own estimation, for the sake of the Hereafter, and leaves many works behind for this worldly life or for their successors. It states: We are recording both types of their deeds.

'And all things We have enumerated in a clear register.' 'Imam' primarily means a leader, guide, and point of reference. From this, the word is also used for a book that serves as a guide and point of reference for everyone. Thus, in verse 17 of Surah Hud and verse 12 of Surah Al-Ahqaf, this word is used for the Torah. Here, it is used for that central register in which every person's deeds will be recorded and according to which everyone will receive reward or punishment. This is a further explanation of the preceding clause—showing that let no one remain in the misconception that We have shown any negligence or indifference in writing down people's deeds and words. It is not so; rather, We have recorded everything in a highly clear register which will present the complete account before everyone.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 13–32

Next, to clarify the end of denying the messengers before the Quraysh, the example of a town is presented. Allah Almighty sent two of His messengers to warn the inhabitants of this town, but they denied them. After this, Allah supported His messengers with a third warner, but the inhabitants of the town did not pay any attention to him either. They attributed the warning signs that appeared to the ill-omen of the messengers and threatened to stone them. Ultimately, Allah Almighty sent a decisive punishment upon them and destroyed them completely.

The Qur'an has not mentioned the name of this town, because of which the question arises: which town is meant by this? Commentators have answered that it refers to Antioch (Antakya) and the messengers mentioned here were not messengers of Allah, but were ambassadors sent by Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him), whom he had chosen from among his disciples and sent to warn the people of Antioch. In all the commentaries available to me, this very tradition is quoted. Although Ibn Kathir (ra) has cast doubts upon it and criticized it, since he did not find any other view, he also adopted it. In my view, this opinion is baseless due to several reasons:

1. The first reason is that the manner in which these messengers are mentioned here, and the capacity in which they presented themselves before the people, makes it clearly evident that they were not ambassadors sent by Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him), but messengers sent by Allah Almighty, and in this very capacity they presented themselves before the people. For example, He says: 'When We sent to them two but they denied them; so We supported them with a third, and they said, "Indeed, we are messengers to you."' Further in this connection, when the people denied them on the basis that they were mere humans like themselves, they said with an oath: 'Our Lord knows that we are messengers to you.' If these people were ambassadors sent by Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him), why did Allah Almighty attribute their sending to Himself with such clarity and emphasis? And if they had invited the people in the capacity of Hazrat Isa's (peace be upon him) ambassadors rather than Allah's messengers, why would the people deny them on the basis of their being human? The deniers of the messengers made their being human an excuse for denial because they claimed to be messengers of God. Upon this, people raised the objection: if God had to send a messenger, would He choose humans like ourselves; why did He not choose His angels or some other higher creation for this office?

2. Second, if this event occurred during the time of Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) and resulted in the destruction of the people of Antioch, it was such a major event that it would have certainly been mentioned in the Gospels and Bible history. However, there is no mention of it in the Gospels, nor is there any allusion to it in history. On the contrary, history suggests that the people of Antioch excelled in believing in Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him).

3. Third, here this event is mentioned before the Quraysh as a well-known event. Thus, 'Qaryah' (town) is prefixed with the definite article of familiarity (Al-if-lam-e-ahd)—meaning 'the town' (Al-Qaryah), not just any town. And the context also requires that a well-known event be mentioned here, because the objective is to warn and frighten the Quraysh, and this objective could be achieved only through a famous event, not through an event of which they were entirely unaware.

4. Fourth, Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) was a messenger specifically to the Children of Israel; therefore, neither did he invite non-Jewish nations himself, nor did he send his disciples to them. Rather, he forbade his disciples from going to non-Jewish nations. His statement is recorded as: 'I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.' Regarding non-Jewish nations, his statement is recorded as: 'The bread I have is only for the children; it is not right to throw it to the dogs.' For this reason, the question arises: whom did he send his disciples to in Antioch? Obviously, he could not send them to non-Jewish nations. As for the Children of Israel, they did not deny Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) on the basis of his being human, so why would they deny his disciples on this basis? They believed in prophets whom they accepted as human; hence, they could not raise this objection. Thus, they raised all kinds of mischief against the prophethood of the Prophet (pbuh), but they did not raise the objection that he was human; and when the Quraysh raised this objection, the Qur'an presented the Children of Israel as witnesses, telling the Quraysh to ask them whether all the prophets and messengers We sent before were indeed human.

5. Fifth, it is mentioned below that as a result of denying these messengers, a decisive punishment came upon them that destroyed them completely: 'It was not but one shout, and immediately they were extinguished' (29). We have clarified the Sunnah of Allah regarding such decisive punishments—that they came only as a result of denying the messengers, after complete establishment of proof. Regarding the Jews, it should also be remembered that no such punishment came upon them as a result of denying Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him) like the punishment that came upon the deniers of previous messengers; rather, Allah Almighty cursed them for this crime until the Day of Judgment. The punishment of the curse is more severe than all punishments, due to which they will remain disgraced and humiliated in the world and face disgrace in the Hereafter as well. We have explained this in the commentaries of Surah Al-A'raf and Surah Bani Isra'il.

The weakness of that opinion is entirely clear from these reasons. Besides these, some other reasons will also emerge under the commentary of the subsequent verses. In our view, 'the town' (Al-Qaryah) points to Egypt, where Pharaoh and his nation were destroyed by Allah Almighty as a result of denying Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them), and the third man of truth. The details of the contexts and proofs of this will follow under the commentary of the verses. Recite the verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'And present to them an example: the companions of the town, when the messengers came to it.' (13)

The pronoun 'them' (lahum) refers to the Quraysh, and the definite article on 'Qaryah' (Al-Qaryah) is an indication that this town was familiar and known to the addressees. The subsequent indications are proofs that this refers to Egypt, whose story has been described in the Qur'an in various styles for the Quraysh to take warning, and with whose states they were familiar. 'when the messengers came to it' points to the fact that the objective is to draw the addressee's attention to the conditions of that time when the messengers were sent to them. It states: draw the attention of these people to the story of that town—showing that the end faced by the inhabitants of that town will also be faced by these people if they adopt their way.

"When We sent to them two but they denied them; so We supported them with a third, and they said, 'Indeed, we are messengers to you.'" (14)

This explains the summary of 'when the messengers came to it'—stating: first We sent to them two messengers, but they denied them; then We supported those two messengers with a third person, and they invited them, saying: 'We have come to you as messengers sent by God, so listen to us and obey.'

'two' obviously refers to Hazrat Musa and Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them). In the history of the messengers, this is the only example where Allah Almighty sent two messengers to a nation simultaneously. The details of the reasons for this special arrangement have passed in the previous Surahs. When the Egyptians denied them, Allah Almighty raised a third person in support of these messengers.

Who is meant by this third person? In my view, this refers to the believer of the family of Pharaoh, whose heroic activities have been mentioned under Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) story here below, and also in other places in the Qur'an, especially in Surah Ghafir. What this man of truth did in the support and defense of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and the courage and selflessness with which he did it, shows that his position in the nation of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was like that of Hazrat Abu Bakr As-Siddiq (ra) in this Ummah. Although he was not a messenger in the technical sense, he dedicated his life to supporting and defending the messengers. Thus, from the words of the Qur'an itself, it emerges that his mention here is not as a messenger, but as a special helper of the messengers.

Below in verse 20, his detailed introduction is also presented by the Qur'an, and his historic speech in the Hereafter in support of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) is also coming.

If you reflect on this speech and introduction, it will support my view.

For instance, here his introduction is presented as: 'And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running.' In exactly the same words, his introduction is presented in verse 20 of Surah Al-Qasas: 'And a man came from the farthest end of the city, running. He said, "O Moses, indeed the eminent ones are conferring to kill you, so leave; indeed, I am to you of the sincere advisers."' This refers to the time when Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) had accidentally killed an Egyptian, and Pharaoh's men were conferring to kill him. At that time, this man of truth informed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) of the enemies' conspiracy and advised him to leave. Following this, Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) migrated to Madyan.

His support and defense of which reference is made here has one example coming below. Besides this, the Qur'an has referred to his various achievements. From verse 28 of Surah Ghafir, it is learned that he was a member of Pharaoh's royal family, but was highly upright. He possessed great sympathy for Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) even before his advent—as is clear from the reference of Surah Al-Qasas quoted above: when he came to know that Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) life was in danger, he did not sit peacefully, but ran from a far-off corner of the city, reached Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and warned him of the danger. Similarly, after Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) claim to prophethood, when Pharaoh and his chiefs decided to kill Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), this man of truth delivered a speech in the full court in support of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), which the Qur'an has quoted in Surah Ghafir. We present this speech in our own words so that some idea may be formed of his status and station, and the nature of his support and defense (ta'zeez) to which the Qur'an has referred here. Keep verses 26–45 of Surah Ghafir before you:

'And Pharaoh said, "Let me kill Moses and let him call upon his Lord. Indeed, I fear that he will change your religion or that he will cause corruption in the land." But Moses said, "Indeed, I have sought refuge in my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account."

And a believing man from the family of Pharaoh who had concealed his faith said, "Would you kill a man [merely] because he says, 'My Lord is Allah' while he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord? And if he should be a liar, then upon him is his falsehood; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you. Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar. O my people, sovereignty is yours today, [you being] dominant in the land. But who would protect us from the punishment of Allah if it came to us?"

Pharaoh said, "I do not show you except what I see, and I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct."

And he who believed said, "O my people, indeed I fear for you [a fate] like the day of the companies—Like the custom of the people of Noah and of 'Aad and Thamud and those after them. And Allah wants no injustice for [His] servants. O my people, indeed I fear for you the Day of Calling—The Day you will turn your backs fleeing; there will be for you no protector from Allah. And whoever Allah leaves astray - for him there is no guide. And Joseph had already come to you before with clear proofs, but you remained in doubt of that which he brought to you, until when he died, you said, 'Never will Allah send a messenger after him.' Thus does Allah leave astray him who is a transgressor and doubter."—Those who dispute concerning the signs of Allah without any authority having come to them—greatly hateful is that in the sight of Allah and in the sight of those who have believed. Thus does Allah seal over every heart [of anyone] arrogant and tyrant.

And Pharaoh said, "O Haman, construct for me a tower that I might reach the pathways—The pathways of the heavens; so that I may look at the deity of Moses; but indeed, I think he is a liar."...

And he who believed said, "O my people, follow me, I will guide you to the way of right conduct. O my people, this worldly life is only [temporary] enjoyment, and indeed, the Hereafter - that is the home of [permanent] settlement. Whoever does an evil deed will not be recompensed except by the like thereof; but whoever does righteousness, whether male or female, while he is a believer - those will enter Paradise, wherein they will be given provision without account.

O my people, how is it that I invite you to salvation while you invite me to the Fire? You invite me to disbelieve in Allah and associate with Him that of which I have no knowledge, while I invite you to the Exalted in Might, the Forgiving. Assuredly, that to which you invite me has no [response to a] call in this world or in the Hereafter, and that our return is to Allah, and that the transgressors are companions of the Fire. And you will remember what I [now] say to you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed, Allah is Seeing of [His] servants."

So Allah protected him from the evils of what they plotted, and a bad punishment enveloped the people of Pharaoh.'

Read this entire speech of this great man. He delivered this speech in the Egyptian parliament (House of Lords) before the chiefs of the government when Pharaoh proposed to kill Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and wanted to gain the consensus of the chiefs for his proposal. Until then, he had concealed his faith, but when this decisive hour arrived, he lifted the veil, and then in the support of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and the defense of truth, before Pharaoh and all the chiefs, what he said, with the proofs he said, and with the courage and selflessness he said, has no parallel except in the history of the prophets and the truthful. Remember also that he was no ordinary person, but a prominent member of the royal family; therefore, they could not attribute his advice to any Israelite bias. Regarding Hazrat Musa and Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them), they raised the allegation that they wanted to incite the Israelites to rebellion, but against this voice of truth that arose from among their own family, what suspicion could they spread among their common people? This great and immortal achievement of this glorious man is referred to by Allah Almighty with the words: 'so We supported them with a third' (fa'azzazna bithalith). He was not a messenger in the technical sense like Hazrat Musa and Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them), but he was indeed their most powerful, most selfless, and most loyal companion; therefore, Allah Almighty gave him the rank of the third of the three.

'and they said, "Indeed, we are messengers to you"': Their stating this in the plural form—'Indeed, we are messengers to you'—does not require that all three of them shared the same rank of messengership; rather, this is said by way of dominance (taghleeb). All members of an embassy do not share the same rank, but since all share in the primary responsibility, they have the right to present themselves in this manner. It should be kept in mind here that the primary messengership belonged only to Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). Hazrat Harun was not the primary messenger, but Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) minister. Similarly, this third man was not a messenger, but the greatest helper of these messengers; but since he had fully taken the responsibility of Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) invitation upon himself, and was inviting his nation to follow Hazrat Musa, Hazrat Harun (peace be upon them), and himself, instead of following corrupt leaders, Allah Almighty counted him in their ranks.

"They said, 'You are not but human beings like us, and the Most Merciful has not revealed anything. You are only lying.'" (15)

This is the same objection that the deniers of every messenger raised against their respective messengers—saying: 'You are only humans like us, so how can you be messengers of God? If God had to send a messenger, He would send some higher creation instead of humans like us.' This objection is repeatedly quoted in the Qur'an from the tongues of the deniers of the messengers. Pharaoh and his chiefs also raised this objection against Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and this same objection was raised by the Quraysh against the Prophet (pbuh).

'and the Most Merciful has not revealed anything...': That is, your claim that God has revealed some scripture or revelation to you is entirely a lie. God has not sent down anything.

"They said, 'Our Lord knows that we are messengers to you, And we are not responsible except for clear notification.'" (16-17)

'Our Lord knows' (Rabbuna ya'lamu) is used in the sense of an oath. We have explained its usage in another place. In response to their denial, they said with an oath: 'We have come to you as messengers sent by God.' Just as in verse 14, 'messengers' (mursaloon) appeared in the plural by way of dominance (taghleeb), similarly here, the plural form is used.

'And we are not responsible except for clear notification': This is a warning from them to the people—stating: 'If you deny us, then do so. Our responsibility from Allah Almighty is only to deliver His word to you in highly clear terms. It is not our responsibility to force it into your hearts. If you do not accept our word, you will face the consequences of it. We will have discharged our duty.'

"They said, 'Indeed, we consider you a bad omen. If you do not desist, we will surely stone you, and there will surely touch you from us a painful punishment.'" (18)

The analysis of the word 'tatayyur' has passed in its proper place. Here, it is used in the sense of taking a bad omen and considering someone unlucky.

That is, instead of taking warning from the calamities that were sent upon the Egyptians during the time of these messengers' invitation to alert them, they declared them to be the ill-omen of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and his companions—saying: 'These calamities are not coming upon us because of our own deeds, as Moses and his companions claim, but they are appearing as a result of this deviating invitation of theirs with which our deities are displeased.' At the same time, they threatened them: 'If you do not desist from spreading this false belief, we will stone you to protect the sanctity of our deities, and besides this, you will have to suffer many other pains at our hands.' This theme has also passed in Surah Al-A'raf:

'And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would be reminded. But when good came to them, they said, "This is for us." And if a bad [condition] struck them, they saw an evil omen in Moses and those with him.' (130-131)

"They said, 'Your evil omen is with yourselves. Is it because you were reminded? Rather, you are a transgressing people.'" (19)

The analysis of the word 'ta'ir' (omen) has also passed in its proper place. Here, it is in the sense of ill-omen. This is the reply of the messengers—stating: 'You look for the causes of your misfortune in others, whereas your ill-omen is with yourselves. Whatever is happening to you is the result of your own beliefs and deeds; therefore, instead of blaming others, look into your own hearts and evaluate your own deeds and beliefs.'

'Is it because you were reminded?': That is, do you declare us unlucky for the crime that we reminded you and informed you of good and evil!

'Rather, you are a transgressing people': That is, this is the height of your rebellion and misfortune—that you cross God's limits to invite dangers upon yourselves, and then, instead of being grateful to God's servants who warn you of these dangers, you blame them for these dangers.

"And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running. He said, 'O my people, follow the messengers. Follow those who do not ask of you [any] payment, and they are [rightly] guided.'" (20-21)

This is the detail of the support of truth by the third warner pointed out in verse 14 above. We have mentioned with reference to the verse of Surah Al-A'raf above that 'a man' refers to that believer of the family of Pharaoh whose mention has occurred in detail in Surah Ghafir, and whose speech we have quoted above. The clear context for this is that in exactly the same words in which his introduction is presented here, it is also presented in Surah Al-A'raf:

'And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running.'

From 'running' (yas'a), it emerges that his sympathy for Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was not merely verbal, but also practical. Sitting in their comfortable rooms, many people praise and appreciate a cause of truth and a man of truth, but there are very few individuals, particularly in the class of chiefs and wealthy, who show practical activity for this truth. But the state of this man of truth was different. His house was at a distant corner of the city, as is clear from the word 'aqsa' (farthest), but whenever he felt that Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) was in danger, he ran, as is clear from the word 'yas'a', reached there, and putting all his family interests and even his life in danger, became a shield for him. The Qur'an has referred to one such occasion here, and the context proves that this is the very occasion when he delivered that speech before Pharaoh which we have quoted above. The subsequent verses support this view.

'He said, "O my people, follow the messengers"': This is a summary reference to the speech he delivered before Pharaoh and his chiefs. By 'messengers' (al-mursaloon), he pointed to the group of messengers. This does not require that he considered himself a messenger in the technical sense. The primary emphasis of his statement is that the people of the nation should follow those whom God has sent for guidance or those who support them, instead of following Pharaoh, Haman, Qarun, and other corrupt leaders.

'Follow those who do not ask of you [any] payment...': This presents two proofs in favor of following the messengers. First, these messengers have no personal interest associated with this invitation; rather, rising above every interest, they are suffering all these pains merely for the guidance of creation. On the contrary, the other leaders who are opposing this invitation of truth are throwing dust in people's eyes merely to protect their own interests.

Second, he gave the proof: 'and they are [rightly] guided.' This proof shows that although a person's lack of self-interest is a testimony to their sincerity, mere sincerity cannot be a proof that they are also upon the truth. For this reason, along with a person's sincerity and lack of self-interest, it is also necessary to see whether their word stands the test of reason and logic. If they are sincere and their invitation is also correct according to reason and logic, then not following them is a misfortune.

Ayah 22-25

The tone of the discourse proves that he said this in reply to the criticism of the nation's leaders. When he openly invited the people to follow the messengers, obviously, the self-interested leaders must have declared him an enemy of the nation and the ancestral religion. In reply to them, he said: 'Why should I not worship Him Who created me and to Whom you will all be returned.' In 'and to Him you will be returned', the second-person plural is used for warning. The meaning is: today you are stopping me with great intensity from the worship of that Lord, but one day everyone has to be presented before Him and you will have to answer before Him.

'Should I take other than Him gods...': That is, how is it possible that at your word I set up such things as deities whose helplessness is such that if God wishes to bring any harm upon me, neither can their intercession be of any benefit to me, nor do they themselves possess the status to save me from that harm!

'Indeed, I would then be in clear error': That is, if I were to do so, it would be such a clear deviation for which I would have no excuse.

'Indeed, I have believed in your Lord, so listen to me.' This is a decisive and fitting reply to the nation—implying: open your ears and listen well that I have believed in your Lord. The meaning is: instead of wasting your time in trying to turn me away from this path, it is better that you also adopt this path which I have adopted and believe in the same Lord in Whom I have believed, because in reality He is your Lord as well. If you try to turn me away from Him, you will only try to turn yourselves away.

"It was said, 'Enter Paradise.' He said, 'I wish my people could know Of how my Lord has forgiven me and made me of the honored.'" (26-27)

After this declaration, obviously, his entire nation must have stood up as his enemy and plotted against him, but Allah Almighty protected him from the evil of their plots, and as a reward for his playing this game of life in the path of defending the truth, he was given glad tidings of Paradise, upon which he expressed the wish: 'I wish my people could know the value of this thing, as a reward for which I achieved this forgiveness and honor.'

There is no explicit mention here of when these glad tidings were given to him.

Reflecting on the context of the discourse, three possibilities emerge:

First, that the wicked of the nation martyred him after this declaration of his, and at that time these glad tidings were given to him.

Second, that after this, despairing of the nation's attitude, he migrated, and at that time he received these glad tidings.

Third, that after this, since his mission was completed, Allah Almighty gave him death with these glad tidings.

Although the first possibility appears stronger in the light of the context of the discourse, at the end of his speech quoted above from Surah Ghafir, it is explicitly stated that Allah Almighty protected him from the evil of the nation's plots. After his concluding words, the protection of his life is mentioned as follows:

'And you will remember what I [now] say to you, and I entrust my affair to Allah. Indeed, Allah is Seeing of [His] servants. So Allah protected him from the evils of what they plotted, and a bad punishment enveloped the people of Pharaoh.' (Ghafir: 44-45)

In the light of this verse, the possibility that he was killed is excluded from the discussion, but the other two possibilities remain, and it is difficult to decide which of these two occurred. Its correct knowledge is with Allah Almighty alone. Only this much is certain: the selflessness he showed in defending the truth was rewarded by Allah by declaring him 'given glad tidings of Paradise' in this very world.

Ayah 28-29

In 'after him' (min ba'dihi), both of the above possibilities are present: after his death or after his migration. This refers to the Sunnah of Allah which the deniers necessarily face once the messenger and his companions leave the nation and migrate. It states: after him, We did not have to send down any army from the sky to destroy his nation, but in a single shout of Ours they were destroyed. The word 'Sayhah' (shout) is an expression for the punishment. 'and We were not to send down [any]' is meant to show the reality that the reason for not sending down any army from the sky is that for such tasks We do not send down Our armies; Our single shout is sufficient.

This reality should also be kept in view: although this punishment primarily came as a result of denying Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and Hazrat Harun (peace be upon him), here Allah Almighty has mentioned it as a result of denying this believing servant. This expresses the grandeur of the companions of the messenger—showing that in the sight of Allah Almighty, they hold such a rank that denying them becomes equivalent to denying the messenger, and results in the same end that denying the messenger does.

A question might arise in someone's mind here: the punishment that came upon the deniers of Hazrat Musa—Pharaoh and his nation—occurred in the form of Pharaoh being drowned in the sea along with his entire army, whereas it appears from this that some such punishment came upon them as came upon 'Aad, Thamud, and other previous nations, of which this believing servant had expressed fear in his speech quoted above from Surah Ghafir.

The answer to this question is that both kinds of punishments came upon Pharaoh and his nation. On one hand, Allah Almighty drowned Pharaoh and his armies in the sea through a cyclonic storm; on the other hand, the punishment of thunder, lightning, and earthquake ruined all the constructions of Egypt and all its gardens and fields. This second punishment is not mentioned in the Torah, but it is mentioned in the Qur'an in highly clear words. For instance, He says:

'And We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people were producing and what they were erecting.' (Al-A'raf: 137)

This is also in accordance with the Sunnah of Allah. The punishment that came upon a nation as a result of denying the messenger cut the root of the nation's national existence. This could not be completed merely by the drowning of Pharaoh and his armies. It could be completed only by a punishment of the kind pointed out in the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-A'raf. It is this very punishment that is mentioned here in the verse under discussion.

Ayah 30

This is an expression of regret over the misfortune of the deniers of the messenger—stating that the condition of the people has always been such: whenever Allah sent a messenger for their guidance, they mocked him, and then faced his bad end. Thus, the way Pharaoh and his nation adopted with their messengers is being adopted today by the Quraysh with their messenger and his companions, and they will necessarily face the same end that their predecessors faced. A messenger comes as the greatest mercy of God, but to mock him... then there is no greater punishment than this.

Ayah 31-32

To show the Quraysh the end of mocking the messengers, attention is drawn to the past—stating that this game they are playing is a highly dangerous game. The nations who played this game were wiped out from this world in such a way that they are never to return. Rather, now the presentation of all of them is to be before Us, and We alone will reckon them.

In 'wa in kullun lamma' (And indeed, all of them), 'lamma' is of the same nature as in Surah At-Tariq: 'Indeed, every soul has a guardian over it.' This 'in' is the lightened 'an' (mukhaffafah), and 'la' is its indicator; since some phonetic gap remains in the sentence, to fill it, it is made 'lamma'. It has no effect on the meaning.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 33–50

Next, the invitation of the messenger and his warnings are supported by the signs of the horizons, and those who are closing their eyes to all these signs of the universe to demand a new sign are warned. It is as if, after the historical testimony above, the discourse is reconnected with the preface, in which it was stated that their primary disease is that arrogance does not let them pay attention to any sign above or below, whereas there is no shortage of signs before them and behind them. After presenting the example of Pharaoh's nation for this attitude of theirs and its end, attention is drawn again to the signs of the heavens and the earth. Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 33-35

First of all, attention is drawn to the signs of the earth. The details of how the revival of the dead earth serves as a proof for the Resurrection, Tawhid, and the descent of divine revelation have passed in the previous Surahs. Here, the aspect of Rububiyyah (lordship) is highlighted particularly, and then the right of gratitude to God is demanded which necessarily lies upon the servants. The essential requirement of this gratitude is that the servants worship their Lord alone, because none of these blessings came into existence by man's own effort, nor did anyone else bring them into existence.

'And a sign for them is the dead land'—the indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Ayah' (sign) is a proof to magnify its status. The meaning is: if these people seek a sign, there is no need for them to go far. For them, a highly great sign is this very earth spread beneath their feet. It is entirely dead, We send Our rain to revive it, and then bring forth grains from it, and upon these grains depends their livelihood. Then, if they reflect, they will know that We have not arranged only grains for their livelihood, but We have also grown gardens of date palms and grapevines and other fruits for them in it, and to keep these gardens lush, We have caused springs to flow in them.

'that they may eat of His fruit and what their hands have not produced. So will they not be grateful?': This states the right of this entire arrangement of lordship—indicating that We made this entire arrangement so that the servants benefit from Our blessings and pay attention to the fact that these have not been produced by their own hands, but are rather the gift of a Merciful, Generous, and Beneficent Lord Who has blessed them with these blessings without any entitlement on their part, so that they may become His grateful and obedient servants. But the state of their ungratefulness is such that they are benefiting from all the blessings, but when they are invited to show gratitude to God, they show arrogance and demand signs.

Regarding the pronoun in 'min thamarihi' (of His/its fruit), commentators differ. Most have considered its referent to be Allah Almighty (meaning: they eat of the fruit bestowed by Allah Almighty and show gratitude to Him). I find some affectation in this interpretation. The style of the discourse of the first-person pronoun has been ongoing from above; therefore, to express this meaning, 'min athmarina' (of Our fruits) or some other similar style would have been used here. In my view, the referent of this pronoun is 'Al-Ard' (the earth) which has been mentioned from above, but for it, the masculine pronoun is used here in accordance with the style which grammarians describe as 'by way of meaning'. In the Arabic language, it is a rule that sometimes the pronoun comes not in accordance with the literal word, but in accordance with its meaning. Several examples of this have passed in the previous Surahs. Here, by using the masculine pronoun for 'Al-Ard' (the earth, which is feminine), it is hinted that the 'good land' (Al-Balad al-Tayyib) is meant. This is because rain falls upon both dry and wet, barren and fertile lands, but only the fertile land becomes fruitful. This theme is expressed in Surah Al-A'raf: 'And the good land - its vegetation emerges by permission of its Lord; but that which is bad - its vegetation does not emerge except with difficulty.' (87).

To understand this explanation, have a look at what we wrote under that verse.

'and what their hands have not produced': This sentence stands as a parenthetical clause, and is a highly important reminder. The meaning is: eat of these fruits, and remember that you are not the ones who brought them into existence, but Allah Almighty is. It is the remembrance of this reality that stirs up the feeling of gratitude within man, and this gratitude is the foundation of the entire religion. Those who are unaware of this reality consider Allah's blessings as the miracle of their own entitlement and capability, the result of which is that instead of becoming grateful servants of God, they fall into arrogance, and this arrogance, by its nature, is the precursor to polytheism and all corruption in the land.

Ayah 36

The word 'azwaj' (pairs) is used in the sense of varieties and kinds, and also in the sense of pairs. Here, it is comprehensive of both meanings. Look at the things that grow from the earth: within them there is variety and diversity, and every thing is observed in the form of pairs. Similarly, look at humans: a great difference is observed in their forms, colors, statures, and languages, and at the same time, the characteristic of being in pairs is found within them. The same is the case with that part of the universe which is beyond our knowledge. Now, stepping forward, reflect on the reality: within its diversity is found the harmony of purpose, and within its opposition is found the compatibility of agreement. This shows that only a single will of the One Allah, Who has no partner, is operating upon this universe. No one else has any share in it. The essence of Allah Almighty is pure from every trace of polytheism, and every single thing in this universe is testifying to this purity of His.

Moreover, it is also clarified that when everything is in pairs, and every thing meets with its pair to fulfill its purpose, it is necessary that this world also has a pair; otherwise, it would remain entirely without purpose and without end, and it is far below the majesty of a Wise Creator to do any futile and purposeless work. Thus, to fill this gap of this world, He has made the Hereafter.

The import of the verse is: this universe, by its very existence, is testifying to Tawhid and the Hereafter, and its Creator is pure from having any partner or from having made this world in vain. 'Subhan' is used for declaring purity. Here, its context is entirely clear.

Ayah 37

It states that if these people reflect, for them the night is also a highly great sign of God's power, wisdom, lordship, and Tawhid. We wrap the cover of light over it, and the bright day appears for them in which they perform their works. Then We strip this cover of light from it, and they are in darkness in which they rest. If We did not wrap this cover of light, they would remain in perpetual darkness, and if We did not strip this cover, they would never get the rest of the night. This coming and going of day and night, and their compatibility for the benefit of creation, is a clear proof that this world is not a battlefield of opposites, but a world made by a single Creator Who uses all its opposites for its collective benefit.

Ayah 38

Attention is drawn to the greatest sign appearing during the day—stating: look at this sun, it revolves upon a specified orbit with full discipline. It is impossible for it to deviate even slightly from its orbit, or for a difference of a minute or second to appear in its discipline of time. Is this a testimony to the fact that whatever is happening in this world is happening automatically, and that there is no managing power behind it? Or is it proof that behind it is a Severe, Powerful, Knower, and Wise Being in Whose hand is the rein of everything, and Who is using everything according to the design He made and the project He determined? The correct answer, according to reason and nature, can only be: 'That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing'—meaning this world is neither a thing that came into existence by accident, nor is it running automatically; rather, it is the miracle of the planning of a Being Who is competent over everything and knows everything.

Ayah 39

It states: the same is the case with the second greatest sign appearing in this world—the moon. It is neither automatic nor independent; rather, We have determined phases for it which it has to traverse every month, until finally, after traversing these phases, it becomes like an old, dry palm branch.

'Urjoon' refers to that branch of a date palm in which clusters grow. These branches, after drying, become curved and adopt exactly the shape which the moon has in its final and initial dates. This comparison points to the reality that the moon which foolish people worship is so helpless in its existence that every month it has to traverse different celestial phases, which makes it dry like a thorn and curved in spine like a dry palm branch.

Ayah 40

That is, the entire system of this universe has been so controlled by its Creator that it is impossible for the sun to exceed its orbit and enter the orbit of the moon to catch it, nor is it possible for the night to precede the day even by a single second; rather, each of them has to revolve within its own sphere. This is a highly clear proof that the rein of every single thing in this universe is in the hand of a single Powerful and Knowing God, and He alone is the sole Master of everything. If anyone else had any share in it besides Him, this world would have been destroyed in the conflict of its opposites. Particularly, this reality is entirely prominent—that the things which are most prominent, and because of whose prominence nations made them deities and worshipped them, are testifying by their very existence that they are highly subjected and ruled over, and it is impossible for them to slip even an inch this way or that way from their sphere of work.

Ayah 41-44

In 'dhurriyyatuhum' (their progeny), 'them' (hum) refers to the addressees as humans, and 'dhurriyyah' is in the sense of progeny. 'Mashhoon' refers to a laden ship. It states: if they seek a further sign, they should observe the fact that for the sea journey of the human progeny, We made this arrangement—that their ships sail upon the chest of the sea carrying thousands of tons of cargo and do not drown.

'And We created for them...': It states that just as We made the ship for the journey of the sea, We made similar things for the journey of the land—such as horses and camels, etc. Particularly, the camel held the status of the 'ship of the desert' in Arabia. In this category are also included those vehicles that have now been invented with the help of science—meaning cars, trucks, buses, airplanes, etc. All these things operate under the laws made by God and taught by Him. Man utilizes these laws to invent various things and benefit from them. For this reason, Allah Almighty has attributed them to Himself, because this is the actual reality.

'And if We should will, We could drown them; then no helper...': 'Sareekh' is used in the sense of cry and also in the sense of the one who comes to rescue. Here, it is suitable in all senses. It states: it is Our mercy that their laden ships run upon the chest of the sea. If We should will, We could drown them along with the ship, and then neither could they cry, nor could anyone come to their rescue, nor could they save themselves from this destruction in any way.

'except as a mercy from Us and provision for a time': It states that whatever man possesses is neither the inheritance of his forefathers, nor the miracle of his own strength and capability, but is merely the favor of God and His mercy with which He has blessed him for a specified time. Ultimately, that time is about to come when Allah Almighty will question him regarding all these blessings—asking whether their gratitude and right were fulfilled or not!

Ayah 45

That is, when they are warned: 'Fear the heaven and the earth that are before you and behind you, lest the earth swallow you or fragments from the heaven fall upon you,' instead of being warned, they turn away and demand the sign of punishment. The apodosis (jawab) of 'Idha' (when) is omitted here due to contextual clarity, and after 'mabaina aydeekum wama khalfakum' (what is before you and what is behind you), the words 'of the heaven and the earth' are omitted. This same theme is expressed in Surah Saba as follows: 'Have they not looked at what is before them and what is behind them of the heaven and the earth? If We should will, We could cause the earth to swallow them or let fall upon them fragments from the heaven.' (9).

Ayah 46

That is, this aversion and denial has so overcome them that whatever sign comes to them from Allah Almighty for their teaching and reminding, it becomes entirely useless. Nothing can affect them.

Ayah 47

This is an example of their indifference and hard-heartedness pointed out in the preceding verse—stating that when they are invited to show gratitude, as in verse 35 above: 'that they may eat of His fruit and what their hands have not produced. So will they not be grateful?', they mock it with arrogance. Thus, when they are told: 'Spend of the provision and favor Allah has bestowed upon you in His way, and help the poor who are in need of help,' they reply to the believers: 'This is your clear error—that you want to feed those whom Allah has chosen to keep deprived. If Allah had willed to feed and clothe them as He did us, was there any shortage with Him? But when He did not do so, it clearly means He willed this for them, and when He willed this for them, we will not show any favor to them to please God, but rather invite His anger!' Presenting this ignorant philosophy, the Qur'an has ignored it without making any comment on it. This is because its cruelty and foolishness are so obvious that there was no need for any comment. The objective is merely to show: when hearts are distorted and intellect is turned upside down, such becomes the state of a person!

Ayah 48-50

That is, they pay no attention to the signs of Allah that are recited to them; their sole continuous demand is: let the punishment of which they are being warned be shown to them. Without this, they are not ready to accept those who give news of it as truthful.

'They do not await except one shout...': The word 'Sayhah' (shout) has passed in verse 29. It states: on what strength are they demanding this punishment with such pomp! God does not have to make any grand preparations for it. His single shout is sufficient for them. It will catch them while they are still in their disputes. The meaning is: if it is not coming, the cause is not that its preparation is not complete. There is no deficiency in its preparation. It is only that Allah Almighty is granting respite to people out of His mercy.

'And they will not be able to make a bequest...': That is, that single shout of God will be such that whoever is wherever will be caught right there. They will not even get enough time to make a bequest to anyone or to return to their families.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 51–67

Next, it is clarified: just as a single shout of Ours is sufficient to bring any punishment in this world, similarly, when We wish to bring the Resurrection, We will not have to make any special arrangements; rather, in a single blowing of the Horn, everyone will stand up.

After this is the depiction of the Day of Judgment—showing that the believers on that Day will be engrossed in their recreations, and the disbelievers will face the results of their deeds. On that Day, no one will be allowed to speak with their tongue, but rather every person's hands and feet will testify against them.

Then a threat is given: 'Since you are demanding the punishment with such audacity, remember that this is not in the least difficult for Us. If We should will, We could transform you in your places in such a way that you would be completely deprived of all your powers and capabilities in the blink of an eye. You can observe its traces in this world, provided you have eyes.' If We have not transformed them, the cause is not that this task is difficult for Us, but rather it is Our mercy that We grant people respite despite their rebellion. Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 51

It has been stated in verses 48–49 above that if the punishment with which they are being threatened in case of denying the messenger is delayed, the cause is not that Allah Almighty has to make some special preparations which are not complete. If He wishes to send the punishment, His single shout is sufficient. Now, it is stated that the same is the case with the Resurrection. To bring it, God will not have to make any special preparations; rather, as soon as the Horn is blown, they will run from their graves toward their Lord. The meaning is: those who are demanding the punishment or the Resurrection to harass the messenger should remember that none of these tasks is in the least difficult for Allah. These are tasks that occur in the blink of an eye. If despite this they are not occurring, the cause is nothing but that Allah Almighty wants people to benefit from this respite to repent and reform themselves.

Ayah 52

That is, these mocking people will beat their heads in panic and terror at that time, and say: 'Who has raised us from our graves!' They will ask this question not to search for reality, but to express their misfortune—implying: we assumed that we would not be raised after death, but this turned out to be a real event!

'[It will be said], "This is what the Most Merciful promised, and the messengers spoke the truth."' This can be a part of their own statement—meaning: this is the very same thing of which we were informed by the Most Merciful, and the messengers' word turned out to be entirely true. It is also possible that this is said by the angels, on behalf of Allah Almighty, to humiliate them at that time—meaning: now beat your heads over your misfortune; this is the very same thing of which the Most Merciful had informed you through His messengers, but you paid no attention to them. My inclination is toward this second view. The reason is that the parallels in the Qur'an clearly support this. In Surah As-Saffat:

'It will be only one shout, and at once they will be staring. And they will say, "O woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense." [It will be said], "This is the Day of Judgement which you used to deny."' (19–21)

In this, the final sentence obviously will be said on behalf of Allah Almighty to humiliate them.

Ayah 53

All of this will occur in the blink of an eye, merely by Our single shout. For this, We will neither have to construct towers nor install cannons. Just as We can bring into existence whatever We will by Our word 'Kun' (Be), We can bring the Resurrection whenever We will by Our single shout. 'Jamee' (all) is for emphasis—implying that let no one remain in the misconception that they can escape from Us. After this, all of them—small and great, leaders and followers, worshippers and deities—will be caught and brought before Us. 'Muhdaroon' (brought/present) is a depiction of their disgrace—showing that they will be brought before Us in the manner that criminals are brought before a court.

Ayah 54

In this verse, the style is of portraying the scene. It is as if that Day is present, and this is being said to the addressees. There are many examples of this in the Qur'an.

It states: today is the Day of the manifestation of perfect justice. Today no soul will be wronged in the least. 'and you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do'—this explains that perfect justice: today the principle upon which people will be dealt with is that everyone will receive in return only what they have done. Obviously, when one's own earning is to appear before them, there is no possibility of any injustice or unfairness.

Ayah 55-58

This is a depiction of the state of the people of Paradise on that Day. It states that on that Day, the people of Paradise will be engrossed in their pure recreations. The indefiniteness (tankeer) of 'Shughul' (recreation) is to magnify its status.

'They and their spouses...': This mentions their wives—stating that they will also join in their recreations. Their mention with special care was necessary because no recreation of a person is complete until their family also joins in it. 'reclining on adorned couches'—this is a depiction of the highest seating arrangement. In ancient times, kings and queens sat reclining on adorned thrones. The people of Paradise will sit in this manner in their eternal kingdom.

'For them therein is fruit...': Fruits will be presented before them in it, along with whatever they demand. 'Ma yadda'oon' means 'ma yashtahoon'—whatever they desire will be present for them. It should be kept in mind that in this world, even the greatest king has not been able to achieve this status, nor will they ever be able to achieve it: that whatever they desire is presented before them immediately. But the people of Paradise will have this status in Paradise, and will have it forever.

'[And] "Peace," a word from a Merciful Lord.' This mentions the greatest honor which the people of Paradise will achieve in Paradise—that peace will be sent to them from the Merciful Lord. In my view, the construction of the sentence is: 'Tahiyyatuhum salamun qawlan min rabbin raheem' (Their greeting is 'Peace,' a word from a Merciful Lord); the subject (mubtada) is omitted here so that all the addressee's attention is focused on the predicate (khabar). In Surah Al-Ahzab, He states: 'Their greeting the Day they meet Him will be, "Peace."' (44). From the Hadiths, it is learned that the angels will enter from every gate of Paradise and deliver the peace of Allah Almighty to the people of Paradise. Who can estimate this honor of the people of Paradise—that they will receive the peace and message from the Merciful Lord!

If I sacrifice my life for these glad tidings, it is entirely justified!

Ayah 59-62

After describing the state of the people of Paradise, this describes the fate of the criminals. They will be commanded: 'O criminals! In the world you remained mixed with My believing servants, because the world was a home of trial; but now the Day of the reward of deeds has arrived. For this reason, separate yourselves from My believing servants today and face the punishment for your deeds. Now your world is different and their world is different.'

'Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam...': When 'Ahada' (enjoin) is followed by the preposition 'ila' (upon), it means to make someone responsible and a guarantor. For example: 'And We enjoined upon Abraham and Ishmael, "Purify My House for those who perform Tawaf..."' (Al-Baqarah: 125). This will be said to the criminals at that time by way of reproach: 'O children of Adam! Did I not make you bound to this covenant—that you do not worship Satan, he is your open enemy, but worship Me alone? This is the straight path, walking upon which you can reach Me. But you turned out to be so unfortunate that he led a vast creation among you astray; did you not possess even this much intellect to distinguish between your friend and your enemy? And remember that through his deception, he turned your father Adam (peace be upon him) out of Paradise, and his enmity with the progeny of Adam is until the Day of Judgment.'

The enmity of Satan, Satan's challenge, Allah Almighty's reminding of the progeny of Adam, and other matters of this series to which reference is made here, have been described in detail in the previous Surahs, particularly Al-Baqarah and Al-A'raf. Understand these summaries in their light.

Ayah 63-64

That is: if you did not remember My covenant and My warning, then its end is now before you in the form of this Hell. Of this, My messenger and My righteous servants warned you, but you continuously denied their words. Now enter it; this is your permanent home.

Ayah 65

In the preceding verses, the style of the discourse was in the second person; in this verse, it changes to the third person. The reason is that in the preceding verses there is reproach, for which the second-person style is suitable, and in this verse there is a depiction of their helplessness, for which the third-person style is more suitable. It states: 'On this Day, We will seal their mouths and make their hands and feet speak, who will relate to Us the story of all their deeds.' The reason for sealing their mouths is that the tongue can speak lies and fabricate excuses, but the hands and feet will relate exactly what they did. In this way, man's own organs will establish proof against him, and this proof will be heavier than all proofs. This theme is also in Surah Ar-Rahman: 'So on that Day no question will be asked of man or jinn as to his sin... The criminals will be known by their marks, and they will be seized by the forelocks and the feet.' (39–41).

Ayah 66

That is, when their state is such that they are not using these capabilities which Allah has bestowed upon them, they deserve that We deprive them of them, and this task is not in the least difficult for Us. If We willed, We could wipe out their eyes, then they would wander in search of the path, but they would not find any way. But We did not do so; this is Our mercy, and still the opportunity remains for them to benefit from this mercy of Ours and not spend their lives closing their eyes. 'Law' (If) comes for a past conditional, and in our view, an auxiliary verb is omitted here before the continuous verb—meaning: 'wa law kunna nasha'u' (and if We had willed).

Ayah 67

Similarly, if We willed, We could transform them in their places so that they could neither proceed forward nor turn backward; but this is Our favor that We did not do so, but have granted them respite so that if they wish, they may regain their senses, recognize the right of the capabilities bestowed by God, value them, and become grateful servants of their Lord.

Ayah 68

The threat given above is supported by proof from the conditions and observations of human life—showing that for Allah Almighty, transforming people's powers and capabilities is not in the least difficult. When a human comes into existence, he is a lump of flesh, then Allah Almighty gradually bestows power and strength upon him until he is full of all the powers and capabilities of youth. Then old age begins to overcome him, and gradually he returns to the same state of weakness and helplessness in which he was at the time of his birth. The Qur'an has drawn the picture of this state in these words: 'Allah is He Who created you from weakness, then He made after weakness strength, then He made after strength weakness and grey hair.' (Ar-Rum: 54). This is the depiction of the general state. Everyone who passes through the stages of youth and old age experiences this. The helplessness of powers and capabilities is experienced even more by those who face the stage of the decrepit age (Ardhal al-Umr). The Qur'an has drawn their picture in these words: 'And among you is he who is returned to the most decrepit old age so that he knows nothing after having known.' (An-Nahl: 70).

In the verse under discussion, attention is drawn to this very situation—stating: in this world you observe that whomever We grant a long life, gradually We return him backward in his powers and capabilities. That is, he is returned backward to the same period of helplessness from which he began his life. This shows that all powers and capabilities are bestowed by God alone; He can take them back whenever He wills, and take them back in such a way that no awareness of his right and left remains to him. So it is a matter of pity for those who do not value Allah's blessings and, upon receiving them, fall into arrogance instead of becoming His grateful servants.

'Tankees' and 'Naks' are considered entirely synonymous by most lexicographers—meaning to return something backward. But in my view, 'Tankees' also contains the meaning of graduation—meaning to return something backward step by step. Both words are used in the Qur'an, and in both places this difference is observed. The word 'Khalq' here is in the sense of 'creation/physique', and in this sense it is used in the Qur'an.

8. Subsequent Theme: Verses 69–73

The subsequent verses are the concluding verses of the Surah. In the conclusion, the theme of the preface is taken up again in a new style. It will be recalled that the Surah began with an oath by the wise Qur'an, and this wise Book was presented as a proof of the Prophet's (pbuh) messengership, and the Prophet (pbuh) was consoled that through this Book Allah Almighty has bestowed a great favor upon the unlettered, but only those will believe in it within whom remains the spark of life. After this preface, the discourse turned toward proving the claims of the Qur'an. Here too, if you reflect, the same point has appeared in a new style. First is the refutation of those opponents who declared the Qur'an to be poetry and the Prophet (pbuh) to be a poet. Then it is clarified what kind of people will be affected by this discourse and what kind will remain deprived of it. After this, referring to some clear signs of lordship, the invitation to gratitude upon which this Surah is based is repeated. After this, the discourse turns toward consoling the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: do not grieve over what people are saying in your opposition; those who do not desist from even making sarcasms against God, if they mock you, it is nothing surprising. Leave their matter to Allah. Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 69

The referent of the object pronoun is the Prophet (pbuh). This is a refutation of the idea of those who declared the Qur'an to be poetry and the Prophet (pbuh) to be a poet. It states: 'We have not taught him poetry, nor is it suitable for him.' This is a reply to those who declared the miraculous eloquence of the Qur'an as merely the miracle of its presenter's poetic magic so that their common people would not be overawed by its influence and accept it as a heavenly book. To turn the common people away from the Qur'an and the Prophet (pbuh), they said: 'The Qur'an is indeed a highly eloquent discourse, but this eloquence is not because it is a heavenly revelation, as its presenter claims, but rather, just as there is influence in the discourse of our high-class poets, there is influence in this person's discourse. For this reason, this person should be considered at most a high-class poet, not a prophet and messenger, and this Qur'an should be given the status of a poetic discourse, not of revelation.' The Qur'an has refuted this idea of theirs in various places in different styles. Particularly, in Surah Ash-Shu'ara', it has highlighted with great detail the difference between a prophet and a poet which can be observed even by an ordinary person. Those who seek details should have a look at it.

'nor is it suitable for him' clarifies the reality that poetry is a thing inferior to the dignity of prophethood. This inferiority has two aspects.

One aspect is that there is a difference of heaven and earth between the traditions and characteristics of the honorable Prophets (peace be upon them) and the traditions and characteristics of poets. In Surah Ash-Shu'ara', three characteristics of poets are mentioned, and all three are contrary to the dignity of prophethood:

* Their first characteristic is stated to be that they are champions only of words, not of deeds. On the contrary, a prophet is himself the first and greatest practitioner of his every word.

* Their second characteristic is mentioned to be that their discourse is a collection of conflicting thoughts. They wander in every valley, whereas the discourse of a prophet is entirely consistent and harmonious.

* Their third characteristic is stated to be that they are followed by those who are astray. On the contrary, the followers of the Prophets have always been the best of society.

Although there are some exceptions to this rule, to which the Qur'an has pointed, they are very few. The general condition is this. For this reason, Allah Almighty protected His Prophets from the touch of poetry.

Another aspect of its being inferior to the dignity of prophethood is that poetry relates entirely to emotions. It is produced by emotions and operates upon emotions. It stirs up good emotions if good emotions are expressed in it, and also incites bad emotions if it is made a source of expressing bad emotions. Beyond this sphere, its existence is entirely useless. The mission upon which the honorable Prophets are sent relates not only to emotions, but to every aspect of human life; for this reason, their actual appeal is to human intellect, which guides in every aspect of life, rather than to emotions. They do not have merely to incite people, but have to nurture them, correct their mistakes, teach them the manners and principles of individual and collective life, and tell them the laws and regulations of government and state. In short, they have to benefit people with the virtues of both this world and the Hereafter. All these tasks, obviously, are beyond the power of poetry. For these tasks, the need is not for a poet's register or collection of verses, but for a clear Book from Allah Almighty. For this reason, Allah Almighty made His prophets holders of His clear Book, not poets.

Here, a question might arise in someone's mind: when poetry is a thing inferior to the dignity of the honorable Prophets and their objective, why did Allah Almighty bestow the Psalms (Zabur) upon Hazrat Dawud (peace be upon him) in the form of poetry? The answer is that the primary guiding Book with Hazrat Dawud (peace be upon him) was not the Psalms, but the Torah. Consider the status of the Psalms merely as a supplement to the Torah, which contains some prayers, supplications, sermons, and wise advices of Hazrat Dawud (peace be upon him). Combined with the Torah, this is indeed a highly blessed treasure of wisdom. But without the Torah, this was not sufficient for that purpose for which the Prophets were sent.

'It is not but a message and a clear Qur'an'—meaning this Qur'an is not poetry, but a great reminder and a clear scripture of guidance so that it serves as a source of reform and guidance for creation. We have explained the meaning of the Qur'an's being a 'Zikr' (reminder) in its proper place. It reminds of all those realities which Allah Almighty has imbued within human nature, reminds of that entire history of guidance which is spread from Hazrat Adam (peace be upon him) to Hazrat Muhammad (pbuh), and also reminds of all those results and consequences which must necessarily happen in this world and the Hereafter if the messenger of Allah is denied.

'a clear Qur'an'—meaning a comprehensive scripture of guidance which contains highly clear, solid, firm, and reasoned guidance for every aspect of life, and explains every reality in such a way that no room for any doubt remains for any fair-minded person. What compatibility does such a reasoned, clear, and disciplined Book have with poetry! In the entire history of the world, which poet has presented a Book of this kind!

Ayah 70

The subject of 'yundhira' (that he may warn) is the Prophet (pbuh). This describes the objective of sending down this clear Qur'an—stating that Allah Almighty has sent down this Qur'an upon His Prophet so that through it he may warn those within whom remains the spark of intellectual and spiritual life. As for those whose hearts are dead, they will not benefit from it, but rather the proof of Allah will be fully established against them and they will not be able to present the excuse before God that no warner came to them, nor will they become guided.

The verb 'indhar' (warning) is used here in its true sense, and 'hayy' (living) refers to those who are living of heart. A warner warns everyone, but his warning is effective only upon those whose nature is alive, because they are the ones who pay attention to the word, as He says: 'You can only warn one who follows the message' (11). Since, in terms of reality, only these people are living, the Qur'an has called them living. Those who are deprived of this characteristic are not living, but dead. Thus, regarding such people, He states: 'and you cannot make hear those in the graves' (Fatir: 22).
