Within this style of expression, there is also consolation for the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: your job is to wake up the living; waking up the dead is not your responsibility. This theme is repeatedly expressed in the Qur'an in different styles.

'The word' refers to that statement which Allah Almighty uttered in reply to Iblis's challenge—stating: 'I will send down My Book and My guidance for the guidance of the progeny of Adam. Then, whoever follows you after this, I will fill Hell with you all.'

Ayah 71-73

To understand the context of these verses, have a look at verses 33–35 of this Surah. Just as there, after referring to the Qur'an's warnings and its invitation to Tawhid, attention was drawn to some signs of lordship, and then the right of gratitude to God was demanded; similarly here, in support of the Qur'an's invitation, attention is drawn to some signs of His lordship, and then gratitude to God is demanded. Thus, the theme of the preface has appeared again in a new style in the conclusion.

'Do they not see that We have created for them from what Our hands have made...': It states: do they not reflect on the fact that We have created cattle for them by Our power and wisdom, and then made them their owners. They exercise full ownership and authority over them and use them in all their needs. In 'from what Our hands have made' (mimma 'amilat aydeena), the same point is expressed in another style that was expressed in verse 35 above with the words: 'and what their hands have not produced' (wama 'amilat-hu aydeehim). The meaning is: these things testify by their very existence that only God's power and wisdom brought them into existence; no one else had the power to bring them into existence or to make them submissive and obedient to man. It is merely the generosity of the Generous Lord that He created them by His power and wisdom and then gave them in the servitude of man. The meaning is: this situation invites man to reflect on whether any responsibility also lies upon him in exchange for this endless favor of nature? The Qur'an wants to draw people's attention to this question and inform them of its consequences, but foolish people call this reminder of reality poetry.

'And We have tamed them for them...': That is, it is merely the lordship and mercy of God that He made these cattle compatible for man's economic needs, and then made them so submissive to man that he uses them as he pleases. If God had not willed, these cattle would neither have been compatible for man's needs, nor could he have made them submissive. After all, how many animals are there in the world that are neither useful for man's needs, nor can he make them his slaves like cattle. Even if he subdues them, they can at most be a burden for him, but they cannot carry his burdens. It states that among these very cattle are some which he uses for riding, and some from which he fulfills his food needs.

'And for them therein are benefits and drinks, so will they not be grateful?': That is, besides riding and food, many other benefits are achieved from them. Their skin, their hair, their bones, their dung—which thing is not useful for man? Similarly, their milk, and the various drinks made from this milk—yogurt, butter, butter-milk—are all delicious, pleasant, and healthy for man. 'so will they not be grateful?': then do these blessings not establish any right upon them, and do they not demand of them that they show gratitude and obedience to that Lord of Whose blessings they are benefiting? These blessings are teaching this lesson to man through their language of state, and the Book of Allah is also reminding of this very lesson. The meaning is: instead of understanding and accepting such an obvious reality, why are you thinking of escaping from it by declaring it poetry!

Ayah 74-75

That is, instead of facing this obvious reality, they have devised backdoors to escape from reality. The right thing was that they recognized the rights and duties of that Lord of Whose blessings they are benefiting and performed them; but what they did is that they invented other deities besides God, and by performing some rituals for them, they are expecting that if any grasp from God occurs, they will escape with their help.

'They are not able to helper...': It states: what help will they be able to provide? Rather, it will be that along with them, these will also be brought before God as their army, and then Allah Almighty will decide what punishment they deserve.

Ayah 76

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: bear whatever they say, do not grieve over it. Let them call you poet, crazy, and fabricator, whatever they say, and remain assured that Allah is well aware of their secret conspiracies and mischief, and also knows what they are saying and doing openly. The meaning is: when God knows everything, why should you grieve! You are doing God's work, so continue doing it, and trust that your Lord is sufficient to deal with your enemies.

Ayah 77-78

'Man' (Al-Insan) refers to the Quraysh who are discussed here. To express indifference, the generic word is used.

'Daraba mathalan' (presenting an example) is used to describe an example or a wise statement, and when the context is suitable, it is also used in the sense of making a sarcasm or passing a remark on someone. Here, it is used in this second sense.

This discourse is reconnected with what was before it from two aspects. It relates to the consolation given to the Prophet (pbuh) in the preceding verse, and is also reconnected with the requirements of God's lordship described to the Quraysh in the verses preceding that.

Its connection with the theme of consolation is: the Prophet (pbuh) is told that when stubborn people are making remarks even against God, and forgetting their own creation, are asking with great pomp: 'Who can give life to dry bones?', if they mock the Prophet and declare him a poet and madman, it is not surprising.

Its connection with the theme of lordship is: lordship, just as it requires gratitude and Tawhid, similarly requires a Day of Account and Reckoning (the explanation of this has occurred in its proper place), but the state of man's ungratefulness is such that despite benefiting from God's blessings, he is not ready to accept that any Day of Account and Reckoning is about to come in which he is to be questioned regarding these blessings, and if he is reminded of it, he stands up as a rival, and completely forgetting his first creation, asks sarcastically: 'Who can give life to dry bones!' It states: tell them that He will give them life Who created them the first time. If He faced no difficulty in creating them the first time, why would He face difficulty in creating them again!

'and forgot his creation' points to the reality that this foolish question can be asked only by one who does not remember his first creation. Whoever remembers his first creation—that Allah Almighty brought him into existence from dust—cannot ask this question; if he does, it means he has stood up to fight with God as His rival.

Ayah 79

'and He is, of all creation, Knowing.' 'Khalq' here is in the sense of creation. That is, Allah Almighty knows all His creations. 'Does He who created not know?' (Al-Mulk: 14). Let no one remain in the misconception that after they are mixed in the dust, He will forget them. He is aware of every single atom of their existence. 'We know what the earth decreases of them, and with Us is a retaining record.' (Qaf: 4).

Ayah 80

That is, do not consider it improbable that life will appear from ashes and dust. The miracle of opposites appearing from opposites is observed by you before your eyes constantly. You take two branches of a green tree, rub them against one another, and produce fire from them. For the God Who has shown this manifestation of His power, what is highly improbable in His producing life from within dust and ashes!... 'Al-Shajar al-Akhdar' (the green tree) refers to those trees that are found in some regions of Arabia, and travelers of deserts have used their branches to produce fire.

Some of the predecessors have given a different interpretation to this verse, but I find some affectation in it. This interpretation is entirely clear. There were certain trees in Arabia from which people produced fire. Their names are also mentioned in books. If these trees are not found now or are very rare, it makes no difference. In this world, deletion and establishment continuously occur by the command of Allah. If the primary addressees of the Qur'an were aware of this, this reasoning is entirely fitting. Its primary foundation is upon the reality that proofs of the existence of opposites from opposites exist in this world, and this is a matter which cannot be denied by anyone. Who does not know that the most powerful fire is found within water?

Ayah 81

This improbability is removed from another aspect—stating: can the God Who created the heavens and the earth, and faced no difficulty in it, not be competent to create the like of them again? Let them reflect: which of these two is the more difficult task? 'Yes, and He is the Knowing Creator.' This answers the question Himself—stating: yes, He is fully competent to create them again. With this answer, there remains no room for denial for the addressee, no matter how many arguments they fabricate. 'and He is the Knowing Creator' (wa huwal-khallaqul-aleem) presents proof for the claim from the attributes of Allah Almighty—that the primary Creator and Knower is God alone. In verse 79 above, He stated: 'and He is, of all creation, Knowing'; this same theme is expressed here in more powerful words—stating that God is the great Creator; let no one remain in the misconception that He became tired by creating this world or that His power of creation has ended; rather, He is as fresh as He was before, and He is fully aware of the constituent parts of His every creation; when He wills, He will restore His every part and joint.

Ayah 82

That is, let no one remain in the misconception that to create something, God has to establish factories, or manage capital, materials, labor, and machines. Allah Almighty is not in need of anything in the completion of His will. His matter is only that when He intends a matter, He commands it: 'Be,' and it is in accordance with His command.

Ayah 83

'Malakoot' means authority and power. This is a warning at the end—stating: the God in Whose hand is the rein of everything, and to Whom you must return, is pure from every defect and blemish; so accept Him as pure from every defect and continuously declare His purity. There is no partner in His essence, attributes, and rights. He is high above every partnership, He has not made this world in vain; for this reason, everyone must be presented before God and answer for their deeds. For Him, raising people again after death and decay is not in the least difficult; He is competent over everything. His knowledge encompasses everything. Nothing is hidden from His eyes, and no word or deed of anyone is concealed from Him.

With the help and guidance of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Praise be to Allah for this.

Rahman Abad: 8 March, 1975 AD.
